title: Malignant Hyperthermia During General Anesthesia in a 19-Year-Old Undergoing Surgery: Recognizing the EARLIEST Sign (an Unexplained Rising End-Tidal CO2) and Masseter/Muscle Rigidity, STOPPING the Triggering Agents, Activating the MH Protocol, Giving DANTROLENE, Cooling, and Coordinating the Emergency Team Response
subtitle: MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA (MH) is a rare, life-threatening, INHERITED (autosomal dominant, usually a RYR1/ryanodine-receptor mutation) HYPERMETABOLIC crisis of skeletal muscle triggered by exposure to specific anesthetic agents: the VOLATILE INHALED ANESTHETICS (sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane, halothane) and the depolarizing neuromuscular blocker SUCCINYLCHOLINE. In susceptible patients these agents cause UNCONTROLLED CALCIUM RELEASE from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, producing sustained muscle contraction and a runaway hypermetabolic state. It usually appears INTRAOPERATIVELY but can occur in the early post-anesthesia (PACU) period. RISK/SCREENING: a personal or FAMILY HISTORY of MH, of problems/death under anesthesia, or of muscle disorders is the key red flag — screen preoperatively and alert the anesthesia team. RECOGNITION (the nurse must know the order of signs): the EARLIEST and most SENSITIVE sign is an unexplained, RAPIDLY RISING END-TIDAL CO2 (EtCO2) that does NOT respond to increased ventilation (hypermetabolism generates excess CO2); TACHYCARDIA is often the first vital-sign change, with tachypnea and unstable/elevated blood pressure; MASSETER (jaw) MUSCLE RIGIDITY — especially right after succinylcholine — and then generalized muscle rigidity are highly suggestive; a mixed RESPIRATORY and METABOLIC ACIDOSIS and hypoxemia develop; HYPERKALEMIA from muscle breakdown causes dysrhythmias; and RHABDOMYOLYSIS produces a markedly elevated CK and MYOGLOBINURIA (dark/cola-colored urine) with risk of acute kidney injury. CRITICALLY, HYPERTHERMIA is a LATE sign (the temperature can then rise extremely fast, more than 1-2°C every 5 minutes, to very high levels) — the team must NOT wait for a fever to act; the rising EtCO2, tachycardia, and rigidity prompt immediate action. EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT (activate the MH protocol — a coordinated team response): (1) IMMEDIATELY STOP the triggering agents — discontinue the volatile anesthetic and succinylcholine; CALL FOR HELP and ACTIVATE the MH PROTOCOL, get the MH CART, designate roles, and call the MH hotline (MHAUS) as needed; conclude/abort surgery as soon as feasible. (2) Deliver 100% OXYGEN at HIGH FLOW and HYPERVENTILATE to wash out CO2 (increase fresh-gas flow; do not delay treatment to change the anesthesia machine). (3) Give DANTROLENE SODIUM — the specific antidote, a skeletal-muscle relaxant that blocks sarcoplasmic-reticulum calcium release — IV as soon as possible: ~2.5 mg/kg rapid IV, repeated every ~5 minutes (titrated up to ~10 mg/kg or more) until the signs (EtCO2, rigidity, tachycardia, temperature) subside; the older formulation (20 mg/vial reconstituted with 60 mL sterile water) is LABOR-INTENSIVE and requires MULTIPLE staff to mix many vials quickly (the newer concentrated formulation reconstitutes faster), so the team is mobilized to prepare it. (4) COOLING measures for hyperthermia — cold IV fluids, surface ice packs to the groin/axillae/neck, cooling blankets, and cold lavage as indicated — STOPPING active cooling around 38°C to avoid overshoot hypothermia. (5) Treat HYPERKALEMIA (calcium, insulin with dextrose, bicarbonate; albuterol) and DYSRHYTHMIAS with standard antiarrhythmics — but AVOID CALCIUM-CHANNEL BLOCKERS, which combined with dantrolene can cause hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse. (6) Treat acidosis (sodium bicarbonate) and protect the KIDNEYS from myoglobin with fluids to maintain urine output (± diuretics/mannitol). (7) MONITOR continuously — EtCO2, CORE temperature, ECG, ABGs, potassium, CK, coagulation (DIC risk), and urine output/color. (8) After the crisis, transfer to ICU for ≥24-48 hours of monitoring because RECRUDESCENCE can occur; continue dantrolene per protocol. PREVENTION/TEACHING: for MH-susceptible patients use NON-TRIGGERING ('clean') anesthesia (total IV anesthesia with propofol, non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers, regional/local techniques) and have dantrolene immediately available; provide patient/family EDUCATION — inform all future anesthesia providers, wear a medical-alert identification, pursue FAMILY screening (autosomal dominant; muscle contracture/genetic testing), and use MHAUS resources. NURSING ROLE: identify risk preoperatively; recognize the earliest signs (rising EtCO2, tachycardia, rigidity) without waiting for fever; immediately help STOP triggers and ACTIVATE the MH protocol; mobilize and coordinate the team to prepare and give DANTROLENE; deliver cooling and treat hyperkalemia/acidosis/rhabdomyolysis; monitor for complications and recrudescence; and educate the patient and family.

patient.name: Tyler M.
patient.code: Full code; goal = recognize malignant hyperthermia early, stop the triggering agents, activate the MH protocol, administer dantrolene, cool and stabilize, and coordinate the emergency team response
patient.admitDate: A previously healthy 19-year-old man is undergoing general anesthesia for elective orthopedic surgery (open reduction/internal fixation of a fractured ankle). Anesthesia was induced with a volatile inhaled agent and succinylcholine. Roughly 30 minutes into the case, the anesthesia provider notes an unexplained, RAPIDLY RISING end-tidal CO2 that does not respond to increased ventilation, along with sinus tachycardia and masseter/generalized muscle rigidity; the core temperature begins to climb. This is a MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA crisis. The perioperative team must immediately stop the triggers, activate the MH protocol, give dantrolene, cool the patient, treat hyperkalemia/acidosis, and coordinate a rapid, role-based emergency response.
patient.room: Operating room → MH emergency response; MH cart and dantrolene mobilized; interdisciplinary team (anesthesiologist/CRNA, surgeon, circulating and scrub nurses, additional staff to reconstitute dantrolene, pharmacy, ICU); transfer to ICU after stabilization for ≥24-48 hours of monitoring for recrudescence

vitals.rr: Ventilated; EtCO2 RISING to 68 mmHg despite increased ventilation
vitals.temp: 37.6°C (not yet high — hyperthermia is a LATE sign)

vitals.rr: EtCO2 continues to climb unresponsive to hyperventilation
vitals.temp: 38.2°C and rising; MASSETER rigidity (after succinylcholine) progressing to generalized muscle rigidity; ABG shows mixed respiratory + metabolic ACIDOSIS; potassium rising — hallmark hypermetabolic MH crisis

vitals.rr: 100% O2 at high flow; HYPERVENTILATING to wash out CO2
vitals.temp: 38.6°C; triggering agents STOPPED (volatile anesthetic + succinylcholine discontinued); MH protocol activated, MH cart at bedside, team mobilized; DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg rapid IV given and repeated q5min; surgery being concluded

40.6°C (rose rapidly); HYPERKALEMIA with peaked T waves/dysrhythmia; DARK/cola-colored urine (myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis); markedly elevated CK — life-threatening complications requiring aggressive treatment
38.4°C and falling with cooling (stop active cooling near 38°C to avoid overshoot)

37.1°C; crisis resolved — EtCO2, rigidity, and temperature normalized on dantrolene; transferred to ICU for ≥24-48 h monitoring for RECRUDESCENCE; renal function/urine protected; family and genetic counseling initiated

labs.n: PRESENTATION + recognizing MH (trigger exposure, EARLIEST signs, hyperthermia is LATE)
labs.v: Tyler M., a healthy 19-year-old, is under general anesthesia (a VOLATILE agent plus SUCCINYLCHOLINE) for ankle ORIF when, ~30 minutes in, the anesthesia provider notes an unexplained, RAPIDLY RISING END-TIDAL CO2 unresponsive to increased ventilation, sinus TACHYCARDIA, and MASSETER then generalized muscle RIGIDITY, with the core temperature beginning to climb. This is MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA (MH) — an inherited hypermetabolic crisis of skeletal muscle triggered by volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine. The NURSE must know the ORDER of signs: the EARLIEST/most sensitive is the rising EtCO2 (hypermetabolism floods the body with CO2); tachycardia is often the first vital-sign change; masseter/generalized rigidity is highly suggestive; and HYPERTHERMIA is a LATE sign that can then rise extremely fast. The team must NOT wait for a fever — the rising EtCO2, tachycardia, and rigidity demand immediate action. RISK: a personal or FAMILY history of MH, anesthesia problems/death, or muscle disorders is the key preoperative red flag. Early recognition and an immediate, coordinated response are what save the patient.
labs.r: MH = inherited hypermetabolic crisis triggered by VOLATILE anesthetics + SUCCINYLCHOLINE. EARLIEST/most sensitive sign = unexplained RISING EtCO2 unresponsive to ventilation; tachycardia often first vital change; MASSETER→generalized RIGIDITY; HYPERTHERMIA is LATE (don't wait for fever). Red flag = personal/FAMILY history of MH/anesthesia problems/muscle disorders → screen pre-op. RN role: recognize the earliest signs and act immediately.

labs.v: MH is usually caused by an AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT mutation (most often in the RYR1 gene encoding the ryanodine receptor) that, on exposure to a trigger, causes UNCONTROLLED CALCIUM RELEASE from the sarcoplasmic reticulum of skeletal muscle. The result is sustained muscle contraction and a HYPERMETABOLIC state: massive oxygen consumption and CO2 production, heat generation, acidosis, and muscle breakdown. TRIGGERS are the VOLATILE INHALED ANESTHETICS (sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane, halothane) and the depolarizing neuromuscular blocker SUCCINYLCHOLINE; non-triggering agents (propofol, etomidate, ketamine, opioids, nitrous oxide, non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers, and local/regional anesthetics) are safe. SCREENING is preventive: ask every surgical patient about a personal or FAMILY history of MH, of adverse/unexplained reactions or death under anesthesia, and of muscle disorders; susceptibility can be confirmed by the caffeine-halothane contracture test or genetic testing. An MH-susceptible patient should receive a NON-TRIGGERING ('clean') anesthetic (total IV anesthesia, regional techniques) with dantrolene immediately available and a prepared, vapor-free machine. Because MH is autosomal dominant, identifying it in one patient has implications for biological relatives.
labs.r: MH = autosomal dominant (RYR1) defect → uncontrolled sarcoplasmic-reticulum CALCIUM release → sustained contraction + hypermetabolism. TRIGGERS = volatile anesthetics (sevo/iso/des/halothane) + SUCCINYLCHOLINE. SAFE = propofol/etomidate/ketamine/opioids/nitrous/non-depolarizing NMBs/regional. SCREEN personal/family history of MH/anesthesia problems/muscle disease (contracture/genetic testing). Susceptible → NON-triggering anesthesia + dantrolene available. RN role: screen risk + ensure trigger avoidance.

labs.n: EMERGENCY TREATMENT — stop triggers, dantrolene, oxygen, MH protocol (coordinated team response)
labs.v: MH is an EMERGENCY managed by ACTIVATING the MH PROTOCOL — a coordinated, role-based team response: (1) IMMEDIATELY STOP the triggering agents (discontinue the volatile anesthetic and succinylcholine); CALL FOR HELP, activate the protocol, bring the MH CART, assign roles, and call the MH hotline (MHAUS) as needed; conclude or abort surgery as soon as feasible. (2) Provide 100% OXYGEN at HIGH FLOW and HYPERVENTILATE to wash out CO2 (increase fresh-gas flow; do not delay treatment to swap the machine). (3) Give DANTROLENE SODIUM — the specific ANTIDOTE that blocks sarcoplasmic-reticulum calcium release — IV ASAP at ~2.5 mg/kg rapidly, REPEATED every ~5 minutes (titrated up to ~10 mg/kg or more) until EtCO2, rigidity, tachycardia, and temperature subside. RECONSTITUTING dantrolene is LABOR-INTENSIVE (the older 20 mg vials each need 60 mL of sterile water and many vials are required), so MULTIPLE staff are mobilized to mix and administer it quickly (the newer concentrated formulation is faster); this team mobilization, role assignment, and closed-loop communication are central nursing-management tasks during the crisis. The circulating nurse coordinates the MH cart, helps prepare dantrolene, documents the timeline, and communicates with pharmacy/ICU/blood bank. Speed matters: outcomes are far better when dantrolene and supportive care are delivered early.
labs.r: MH protocol (coordinated team): (1) STOP triggers (volatile + succinylcholine) + CALL FOR HELP + MH cart + MHAUS hotline + end surgery; (2) 100% O2 high-flow + HYPERVENTILATE (don't delay to change machine); (3) DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg rapid IV, repeat q5min up to ~10 mg/kg until signs subside — reconstitution is labor-intensive → MOBILIZE MULTIPLE staff. Circulating RN coordinates cart/dantrolene prep/documentation/communication. Early treatment = better outcomes. RN role: stop triggers + activate protocol + coordinate team + give dantrolene.

labs.n: Complications + supportive care (cooling, hyperkalemia, dysrhythmias, acidosis, rhabdo; recrudescence)
labs.v: Alongside dantrolene, the team treats the hypermetabolic complications: COOLING for hyperthermia — cold IV fluids, surface ICE PACKS to the groin/axillae/neck, cooling blankets, and cold lavage as indicated — STOPPING active cooling around 38°C to avoid overshoot hypothermia. HYPERKALEMIA (from muscle breakdown) is treated with CALCIUM (membrane stabilization), INSULIN plus DEXTROSE, sodium BICARBONATE, and albuterol, with cardiac monitoring. DYSRHYTHMIAS are treated with standard antiarrhythmics and ACLS as needed — but CALCIUM-CHANNEL BLOCKERS are AVOIDED because given with dantrolene they can cause severe hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse. Metabolic ACIDOSIS is treated with sodium bicarbonate guided by ABGs. RHABDOMYOLYSIS releases myoglobin (dark/cola-colored urine) that can cause acute kidney injury, so the team gives FLUIDS to maintain a good URINE OUTPUT (± mannitol/diuretics) to protect the kidneys, and monitors CK. Watch for DISSEMINATED INTRAVASCULAR COAGULATION (a grave late sign). MONITORING is continuous: EtCO2, CORE temperature, ECG, ABGs, potassium, CK, coagulation, and urine output/color. After the acute crisis, the patient is transferred to the ICU for at least 24-48 HOURS because RECRUDESCENCE (a recurrence of the crisis) can occur, with dantrolene continued per protocol.
labs.r: Supportive care: COOL (cold IV fluids/ice to groin-axillae-neck/blankets; STOP near 38°C). Treat HYPERKALEMIA (calcium, insulin+dextrose, bicarb, albuterol) + dysrhythmias — but AVOID CALCIUM-CHANNEL BLOCKERS with dantrolene (→ hyperkalemia/collapse). Treat acidosis (bicarb); protect kidneys from myoglobin (RHABDO) with fluids → urine output ± mannitol; watch DIC. Monitor EtCO2/core temp/ECG/ABG/K/CK/coags/urine. ICU ≥24-48h — RECRUDESCENCE risk; continue dantrolene. RN role: cooling + treat hyperkalemia/acidosis/rhabdo + monitor.

labs.n: Prevention/teaching + nursing role; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PREVENTION + EDUCATION close the loop: for an MH-susceptible patient, use a NON-TRIGGERING ('clean') anesthetic — total IV anesthesia (propofol), non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers, regional/local techniques — with a vapor-free machine and dantrolene immediately available. Teach the patient and family to INFORM ALL future anesthesia/surgery/dental providers, to wear a MEDICAL-ALERT identification, and (because MH is AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT) to pursue FAMILY screening and genetic/contracture testing; connect them to MHAUS resources. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT wait for a fever before acting (hyperthermia is a late sign); did NOT continue the triggering agents or delay treatment to change the anesthesia machine; did NOT give a calcium-channel blocker with dantrolene; did NOT under-dose or delay dantrolene; and did NOT discharge the patient from the PACU/OR without ICU monitoring for recrudescence. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: preoperative risk screening; immediate recognition of the earliest signs (rising EtCO2, tachycardia, rigidity); rapid trigger discontinuation and MH-protocol activation; early, adequately dosed dantrolene delivered by a mobilized team; effective cooling and treatment of hyperkalemia/acidosis/rhabdomyolysis; and ICU monitoring with family/genetic counseling. EXPECTED outcome: with early recognition and aggressive, coordinated treatment, the crisis is reversed, complications are prevented or managed, and the patient and family are protected for the future. NURSING ROLE: screen risk preoperatively; recognize MH early without waiting for fever; help stop triggers and activate the MH protocol; mobilize/coordinate the team to prepare and give dantrolene; provide cooling and treat complications; monitor for recrudescence; and educate the patient and family.
labs.r: PREVENTION: MH-susceptible → NON-triggering anesthesia (TIVA/propofol, non-depolarizing NMBs, regional) + vapor-free machine + dantrolene available. EDUCATE: inform all future providers, medical-alert ID, FAMILY screening (autosomal dominant), MHAUS. NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't wait for fever, didn't continue triggers/delay for machine swap, NO calcium-channel blocker with dantrolene, didn't under-dose/delay dantrolene, didn't skip ICU monitoring. PROTECTIVE: pre-op screening, early recognition, fast trigger stop + protocol, early dantrolene, cooling/complication care, ICU + counseling. RN role: screen + recognize + treat + coordinate + educate.

nursesNote: OR → MH EMERGENCY — Tyler M., 19, healthy, GA (VOLATILE agent + SUCCINYLCHOLINE) for ankle ORIF. ~30 min in: unexplained RAPIDLY RISING EtCO2 (68→climbing) unresponsive to increased ventilation, sinus TACHYCARDIA (often first vital change), MASSETER → generalized muscle RIGIDITY, core temp beginning to climb → MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA (inherited hypermetabolic crisis, autosomal dominant RYR1; uncontrolled SR calcium release). HYPERTHERMIA is LATE — did NOT wait for fever; acted on rising EtCO2/tachycardia/rigidity. RED FLAG screen = personal/FAMILY history of MH/anesthesia problems/muscle disease. EMERGENCY MH PROTOCOL (coordinated team): (1) STOP triggers (discontinue volatile + succinylcholine) + CALL FOR HELP + activate protocol + MH CART + assign roles + MHAUS hotline + conclude surgery; (2) 100% O2 high-flow + HYPERVENTILATE to wash out CO2 (don't delay to change the machine); (3) DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg rapid IV, REPEAT q5min titrated to ~10 mg/kg+ until EtCO2/rigidity/tachycardia/temp subside — reconstitution labor-intensive (old 20mg vials + 60mL sterile water each) → MOBILIZE MULTIPLE staff; circulating RN coordinates cart/dantrolene prep/timeline documentation/pharmacy-ICU communication + closed-loop. COMPLICATIONS/supportive: COOL (cold IV fluids, ice to groin/axillae/neck, cooling blanket, lavage; STOP ~38°C to avoid overshoot); HYPERKALEMIA → calcium + insulin/dextrose + bicarb + albuterol + cardiac monitor; dysrhythmias per ACLS but AVOID CALCIUM-CHANNEL BLOCKERS with dantrolene (→ hyperK/collapse); acidosis → bicarb; RHABDO (dark/cola urine, high CK) → fluids to maintain urine output ± mannitol to protect kidneys; watch DIC. MONITOR EtCO2/core temp/ECG/ABG/K/CK/coags/urine. ICU ≥24-48h — RECRUDESCENCE risk; continue dantrolene. PREVENTION/TEACH: susceptible → NON-triggering anesthesia (TIVA/propofol, non-depolarizing NMBs, regional) + vapor-free machine + dantrolene available; inform all future providers, MEDICAL-ALERT ID, FAMILY screening (autosomal dominant), MHAUS. NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't wait for fever, didn't continue triggers/delay for machine swap, no CCB with dantrolene, didn't under-dose/delay dantrolene, didn't skip ICU monitoring. Crisis reversed; family/genetic counseling initiated.
providerOrders: EMERGENCY: intraoperative malignant hyperthermia — activate the MH protocol; full code

STOP TRIGGERS: discontinue the volatile inhaled anesthetic and succinylcholine immediately; conclude/abort surgery as soon as feasible; call for help and bring the MH cart; call the MH hotline (MHAUS) as needed
OXYGENATION: 100% oxygen at high flow; hyperventilate to eliminate CO2 (increase fresh-gas flow; do not delay treatment to change the anesthesia machine)

DANTROLENE: dantrolene sodium 2.5 mg/kg rapid IV; repeat every 5 minutes (titrate up to ~10 mg/kg or more) until EtCO2, rigidity, tachycardia, and temperature subside; mobilize additional staff to reconstitute and administer
COOLING: cold IV fluids, surface ice packs (groin/axillae/neck), cooling blanket, and cold lavage as indicated; STOP active cooling near 38°C to avoid overshoot hypothermia

HYPERKALEMIA/DYSRHYTHMIAS: treat hyperkalemia (calcium, insulin with dextrose, sodium bicarbonate, albuterol); treat dysrhythmias with standard antiarrhythmics/ACLS — AVOID calcium-channel blockers (dangerous with dantrolene)
ACIDOSIS/RENAL: sodium bicarbonate per ABG; IV fluids to maintain urine output (± mannitol/diuretics) to protect kidneys from myoglobinuria (rhabdomyolysis)
MONITORING: continuous EtCO2, core temperature, ECG, and pulse oximetry; serial ABGs, potassium, CK, coagulation studies (DIC); strict intake/output and urine color
DISPOSITION: transfer to ICU after stabilization for at least 24-48 hours (recrudescence risk); continue dantrolene per protocol

PREVENTION/FUTURE CARE: document MH event; for any future anesthesia use non-triggering agents (total IV anesthesia, non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers, regional) with a vapor-free machine and dantrolene available

EDUCATION: instruct patient/family to inform all future anesthesia/surgical/dental providers, obtain medical-alert identification, and pursue family screening/genetic testing (autosomal dominant); refer to MHAUS resources

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this intraoperative emergency?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: An unexplained, rapidly rising end-tidal CO2 unresponsive to increased ventilation — the earliest, most sensitive sign of malignant hyperthermia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rising EtCO2 is the earliest MH sign and drives immediate action. RN role: recognize the earliest sign.

opts.text: Sinus tachycardia (often the first vital-sign change) with masseter and then generalized muscle rigidity, especially after succinylcholine
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — tachycardia and rigidity are key early/suggestive signs. RN role: recognize the MH pattern.

opts.text: Exposure to triggering agents — a volatile inhaled anesthetic and succinylcholine — in this case
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — trigger exposure is the precipitant; stopping it is the first treatment. RN role: identify and stop the triggers.

opts.text: Hyperthermia is a LATE sign — the team must act on the early signs and not wait for a fever
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — waiting for fever delays life-saving treatment. RN role: act before hyperthermia appears.

opts.text: Anticipated complications — mixed acidosis, hyperkalemia, dysrhythmias, and rhabdomyolysis (dark urine, elevated CK)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these complications must be monitored and treated. RN role: anticipate and address complications.

opts.text: The need to immediately activate the MH protocol — call for help, get the MH cart, and mobilize the team to prepare dantrolene
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a coordinated, role-based protocol response is essential. RN role: activate the protocol + coordinate the team.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — documented for the perioperative record
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue in this crisis.

opts.text: Because the temperature is still near-normal, continue the anesthetic and simply observe, treating only if a high fever develops
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Hyperthermia is a LATE sign; waiting for fever before stopping triggers and treating costs critical time and worsens outcomes.

opts.text: Treat the rising heart rate with a calcium-channel blocker while continuing the volatile anesthetic
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The triggers must be stopped, not continued; and calcium-channel blockers are contraindicated with dantrolene (risk of hyperkalemia/cardiovascular collapse).

opts.text: MH is an inherited (autosomal dominant) hypermetabolic crisis of skeletal muscle from uncontrolled sarcoplasmic-reticulum calcium release, triggered by volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine
opts.rat: CORRECT — etiology and triggers. Foundation for recognition and prevention.

opts.text: The earliest, most sensitive sign is a rising end-tidal CO2; hyperthermia is a late sign
opts.rat: CORRECT — the order of signs. Foundation for early recognition.

opts.text: Dantrolene sodium is the specific antidote, given rapidly IV and repeated until signs subside
opts.rat: CORRECT — definitive pharmacologic treatment. Foundation for management.

opts.text: Management includes stopping triggers, 100% oxygen with hyperventilation, cooling, and treating hyperkalemia, acidosis, and rhabdomyolysis
opts.rat: CORRECT — the supportive components of the MH protocol. Foundation for comprehensive care.

opts.text: Calcium-channel blockers should be avoided with dantrolene because together they can cause hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse
opts.rat: CORRECT — a key drug-safety caution in MH. Foundation for safe treatment.

opts.text: MH-susceptible patients should receive non-triggering anesthesia, and a personal/family history is screened preoperatively
opts.rat: CORRECT — prevention and screening principle. Foundation for avoiding future crises.

opts.text: Hyperthermia is the earliest sign of MH, so treatment should be withheld until the temperature is clearly elevated
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Hyperthermia is LATE; the earliest sign is a rising EtCO2, and treatment must begin immediately on the early signs.

opts.text: The triggering volatile anesthetic should be continued during treatment to keep the patient anesthetized
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The triggering agents must be STOPPED immediately; anesthesia is maintained with non-triggering agents if needed.

opts.text: Stop the triggering agents and activate the MH protocol (call for help, MH cart, mobilize the team)
opts.rat: #1 — Removing the trigger and activating the coordinated response is the first and most urgent step. RN role: stop triggers + activate protocol.

opts.text: Give 100% oxygen with hyperventilation and administer dantrolene rapidly, repeating until signs subside
opts.rat: #2 — Oxygenation and the antidote directly reverse the hypermetabolic crisis. RN role: oxygenate + dantrolene.

opts.text: Initiate cooling measures for hyperthermia (stopping near 38°C)
opts.rat: #3 — Active cooling counters the dangerous, rapidly rising temperature. RN role: cooling.

opts.text: Treat hyperkalemia, dysrhythmias, and acidosis, and protect the kidneys from myoglobinuria
opts.rat: #4 — Managing the metabolic complications prevents arrest and renal injury. RN role: treat complications.

opts.text: Transfer to ICU for monitoring of recrudescence and provide patient/family education and family screening
opts.rat: #5 — Post-crisis monitoring and education protect the patient and relatives going forward. RN role: ICU monitoring + education.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this MH crisis.

opts.text: Immediately stop the volatile anesthetic and succinylcholine, call for help, activate the MH protocol, and bring the MH cart
opts.rat: First and most urgent step — remove the trigger and mobilize the response. RN role: stop triggers + activate protocol.

opts.text: Provide 100% oxygen at high flow and hyperventilate to eliminate CO2
opts.rat: Counters hypoxemia and washes out the excess CO2 of hypermetabolism. RN role: oxygenate/hyperventilate.

opts.text: Administer dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg rapid IV and repeat every 5 minutes until signs subside, mobilizing staff to reconstitute it quickly
opts.rat: Definitive antidote; reconstitution is labor-intensive, so the team is mobilized. RN role: prepare/give dantrolene.

opts.text: Begin cooling (cold IV fluids, ice packs to groin/axillae/neck, cooling blanket), stopping near 38°C
opts.rat: Treats the dangerous hyperthermia while avoiding overshoot hypothermia. RN role: cooling.

opts.text: Treat hyperkalemia (calcium, insulin/dextrose, bicarbonate) and dysrhythmias, and give fluids to protect the kidneys from myoglobin
opts.rat: Manages the metabolic complications and prevents renal injury. RN role: treat complications.

opts.text: Transfer to ICU for at least 24-48 hours of monitoring for recrudescence and continue dantrolene per protocol
opts.rat: Recrudescence can occur; continued monitoring and dantrolene are required. RN role: ICU monitoring.

opts.text: Continue the volatile anesthetic and wait for a high fever before treating
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the trigger must be stopped immediately and treatment begun on the early signs; hyperthermia is a late sign.

opts.text: Give a calcium-channel blocker together with dantrolene to control the heart rate
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — calcium-channel blockers with dantrolene can cause hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse; they are contraindicated.

opts.text: Delay dantrolene to first change out the entire anesthesia machine
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — do not delay treatment; increase fresh-gas flow/high-flow oxygen and give dantrolene immediately rather than waiting to swap the machine.

opts.text: After the signs resolve, send the patient directly home from the PACU without further monitoring
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — recrudescence can occur; the patient requires ICU monitoring for ≥24-48 hours with continued dantrolene.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's/team's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop the triggering agents, call for help, and activate the MH protocol (bring the MH cart, assign roles)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Removing the trigger and mobilizing the team is the immediate priority. RN role: stop triggers + activate protocol.

opts.text: Deliver 100% oxygen with hyperventilation and administer dantrolene rapidly, repeating until signs subside
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Oxygenation and the antidote reverse the crisis. RN role: oxygenate + dantrolene.

opts.text: Initiate active cooling for hyperthermia, stopping near 38°C
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Cooling counters the rapidly rising temperature. RN role: cooling.

opts.text: Treat hyperkalemia, dysrhythmias, and acidosis and maintain urine output to protect the kidneys
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Managing complications prevents arrest and renal failure. RN role: treat complications.

opts.text: Transfer to ICU for recrudescence monitoring; document the event and provide patient/family education and screening
opts.rat: #5 — After stabilization. Monitoring, documentation, and education protect the patient and family. RN role: ICU + education.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After stopping triggers, hyperventilating, and giving dantrolene, the end-tidal CO2 falls, the rigidity relaxes, and the heart rate slows
opts.rat: The hallmark response to correct MH treatment. Expected, desired improvement.

opts.text: Active cooling brings the temperature down and is stopped near 38°C to avoid overshoot hypothermia
opts.rat: Effective, appropriately limited cooling. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Hyperkalemia is corrected and dysrhythmias resolve with treatment; urine output is maintained and stays clear, protecting the kidneys
opts.rat: Successful management of metabolic complications and renal protection. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: In the ICU the patient remains stable on continued dantrolene with no recurrence, and family screening/education is arranged
opts.rat: Stable recovery with recrudescence prevented and future protection. Ideal outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the team waited for a fever before acting; the EtCO2 and temperature climbed further and the patient deteriorated
opts.rat: ⚠️ Waiting for the late sign delayed treatment and worsened the crisis. Demonstrates acting on the earliest signs.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the volatile anesthetic was continued and dantrolene was delayed to change the machine; rigidity, hyperthermia, and acidosis worsened
opts.rat: ⚠️ Continuing the trigger and delaying dantrolene worsened the crisis. Demonstrates stopping triggers and giving dantrolene without delay.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — a calcium-channel blocker was given with dantrolene; the patient developed severe hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse
opts.rat: ⚠️ The contraindicated combination caused life-threatening deterioration. Demonstrates avoiding calcium-channel blockers with dantrolene.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the patient was sent home from the PACU after the signs resolved; the crisis recrudesced hours later without monitoring
opts.rat: ⚠️ Skipping ICU monitoring missed recrudescence. Demonstrates the need for ≥24-48 hours of monitoring.

opts.text: Immediately after the first dantrolene dose, before it takes full effect, the EtCO2 and rigidity are still present and essentially unchanged
opts.rat: Before the antidote takes full effect, an unchanged baseline is expected; continue repeating dantrolene and supportive care and reassess.

opts.text: At discharge the patient verbalizes the need to inform future providers, wear a medical-alert ID, and have family screened for MH
opts.rat: Effective education protecting the patient and relatives. Ideal longer-term outcome.
